did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed ECRNet, a hybrid deep learning network combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformer-based attention mechanisms (specifically an Enhanced Visual Context module and a CCPA feature fusion module with coordinate and channel attention) for skin cancer image recognition. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets to classify skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents the development and evaluation of a novel deep learning model named ECRNet for skin cancer identification. It includes original experimental work, specifically the design of an Enhanced Visual Context (EVC) module and a CCPA feature fusion block, and reports quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) from extensive experiments conducted on the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets. The structure includes standard research sections such as Introduction, Related Work, Materials and Methods, Results, and Ablation Experiments, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: ECRNet: Hybrid network for skin cancer identification
brief_summary: This study proposes ECRNet, a hybrid deep learning model for skin cancer image recognition that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformer-based attention mechanisms to capture both global and local features. The architecture integrates an Enhanced Visual Context (EVC) module for explicit vision center extraction and a CCPA feature fusion block utilizing coordinate and channel attention. The model was evaluated on the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score as metrics. On the ISIC2018 dataset, ECRNet achieved an accuracy of 92.12%, precision of 88.81%, recall of 86.05%, and an F1 score of 87.23%. On the XJUSK dataset, the model attained an accuracy of 86.22%, precision of 83.96%, recall of 81.54%, and an F1 score of 82.09%. The results demonstrate that ECRNet outperforms baseline models and other state-of-the-art methods in skin cancer identification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer identification, specifically mentioning melanoma as an aggressive form of skin cancer in the introduction. The experiments on the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets are for classifying skin lesions, with the general task described as 'skin cancer recognition' or 'skin cancer identification'. The paper does not list specific histological subtypes (e.g., BCC, SCC) as distinct experimental classes, but rather treats the task as identifying malignant skin lesions/cancer, with melanoma being the primary malignant example cited.

The introduction mentions that dermoscopy helps determine whether skin lesions are 'malignant or benign'. The ISIC2018 dataset, used for evaluation, contains both malignant and benign classes (typically including melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and benign keratosis-like lesions), although the paper does not explicitly list the specific benign class labels used in the multi-class classification task.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer identification (malignant vs. benign or specific lesion classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on 'skin cancer recognition' and 'skin cancer identification' using the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (malignant/benign or specific lesion types) as evidenced by the use of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score metrics derived from confusion matrices (TP, TN, FP, FN). The abstract and introduction explicitly state the goal is to classify skin cancer images and determine if lesions are malignant or benign.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset obtained through the ISIC archive, used for skin cancer recognition.

use.training: Used for training and evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.
use.validation: Used for evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.

datasets_used.name: XJUSK
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset comprising non-dermoscopic dermatological images collected to assess the method's performance in dermatological identification tasks.

use.training: Used for training and evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.
use.validation: Used for evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the ECRNet model and baseline models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of ECRNet against baseline models (e.g., ResNet101) and ablated versions (+CCPA, +EVC) by calculating the absolute percentage increase in Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score.
To compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score using True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) derived from the test sets.
To partition the ISIC2018 and XJUSK datasets into 70% training, 20% validation, and 10% test sets to ensure fair evaluation and prevent data leakage.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute percentage improvements in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score against baseline models.

ISIC2018.notes: ECRNet outperformed the baseline model (ResNet101) with improvements of 1.19% in accuracy, 1.96% in precision, 4.08% in recall, and 3.28% in F1 score. The addition of the CCPA module to ResNet101 increased Precision by 0.18%, Recall by 0.63%, and F1 Score by 0.47%. The addition of the EVC module increased ACC by 0.79%, Precision by 0.11%, Recall by 2.59%, and F1 Score by 1.61%.

XJUSK.notes: ECRNet achieved the best results in accuracy, F1 score, and recall compared to other models. Compared to the baseline model (ResNet101), ECRNet increased ACC by 2.25%, Precision by 1.26%, Recall by 3.05%, and F1 Score by 2.15%. The addition of the CCPA module to ResNet101 increased ACC by 0.65%, Precision by 0.96%, Recall by 0.4%, and F1 Score by 0.57%. The addition of the EVC module increased ACC by 0.88%, Precision by 0.28%, Recall by 0.6%, and F1 Score by 0.6%. Convnext-tiny_EVC_CCPA showed high accuracy and precision but low recall and F1 score, attributed to uneven sample distribution and high noise in non-dermoscopy images.

modules: EVC (Enhanced Visual Context) module: Consists of two parallel connected blocks (MLP and LCV) with feature maps connected along the channel dimension.
CCPA (Coordinated Channel and Position Attention) module: Integrates coordinate attention and channel attention mechanisms with Partial Convolution (PConv) for feature extraction.
Residual module: Uses residual connections to handle vanishing/exploding gradients.
architecture_details.design_philosophy: Hybrid network combining CNNs and Transformer-based attention mechanisms to capture both global and local features.

training_details.hardware: Intel i5 12600KF CPU, NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU (24 GB VRAM)

classification_or_output_head.type: Standard classification head (implied by ResNet backbone and metrics)

preprocessing_techniques: Data splitting into 70% training, 20% validation, and 10% test sets
conclusions: The proposed ECRNet hybrid network, which combines CNNs with Transformer-based attention mechanisms (specifically an Enhanced Visual Context module and a CCPA feature fusion block), effectively captures both global and local features for skin cancer identification.
ECRNet outperformed baseline models and other state-of-the-art methods on the ISIC2018 dataset, achieving an accuracy of 92.12%, precision of 88.81%, recall of 86.05%, and an F1 score of 87.23%.

ECRNet achieved the best results in accuracy, F1 score, and recall on the XJUSK dataset compared to other tested models, with an accuracy of 86.22%, precision of 83.96%, recall of 81.54%, and an F1 score of 82.09%.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the individual components, showing that the addition of the CCPA module and the EVC module to the baseline ResNet101 model yielded incremental improvements in performance metrics on both datasets.
The integration of CCPA and EVC modules improved performance across various backbone models (RegNet-12GF, HRNet-W18, Convnext-tiny), demonstrating the generalizability of the proposed modules.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the XJUSK dataset, noting that non-dermoscopic images had higher noise levels and uneven sample distribution, which can negatively impact model performance (e.g., causing high accuracy but low recall in some configurations like Convnext-tiny_EVC_CCPA due to potential bias toward majority classes).
important_remarks: The paper is an unedited author's version accepted for publication in IEEE Access; content may change prior to final publication.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely on absolute percentage improvements.

The XJUSK dataset is a private clinical dataset of non-dermoscopic images with high noise levels and uneven sample distribution, which limits generalizability and may introduce bias toward majority classes.
Data leakage risk exists as the paper does not explicitly detail patient-level separation between training, validation, and test sets, relying only on image-level splits (70/20/10).
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may limit model robustness compared to studies using augmentation.
The authors acknowledge that the model's depth is insufficient, limiting its accuracy and precision, and plan to investigate deeper architectures in future work.
The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets, relying solely on ISIC2018 (public) and XJUSK (private).
Class imbalance is evident in the ISIC2018 dataset (e.g., DF: 115 vs NV: 6705), which may skew performance metrics despite the use of F1 score.
